# Ann McCrackin (Patents and Law)

<!-- vimeo: 1111505175 | track: English (auto-generated) -->

<!-- vid-ref:1111505175 -->
[▶ Watch on Vimeo](https://vimeo.com/1111505175)


Imagine that you are a patent attorney and your client has filed a patent application for an invention related to a fire hydrant locating device that helps firefighters locate fire hydrants more quickly, quickly when they come onto the scene of a fire. However, the US Patent examiner has rejected your client's patent application. So now it is your job to try to persuade the patent examiner to grant a patent for your client. Negotiating with examiners is a critical skill for patent attorneys. This is a skill that I teach in my Patent Practice two course at the University of New Hampshire, Franklin Pierce School of Law. I'm Anne McCracken, I'm a patent attorney myself. I am also a faculty member at UNH and I've been teaching this type of skill with traditional role play exercises for many, many years. But it is not the best way to do it. It's pretty artificial, you know, the student experiences, uh, kind of fake because they don't really know how examiners behave if they're playing the role of the examiner and they're, you know, with their buddies. So they're not really hard on the other party in the negotiation and only half of 'em even get to be in the shoes of the patent attorney because the other half have to be the examiners and they don't get expert feedback from me because I can't be in all of these simultaneously. However, this year I finally found a way to solve all of those issues and create a much more realistic exercise using chat GPT. So let me tell you how I did it. I created a custom GPT that plays the role of the examiner and I had students interact with it using advanced voice mode. So what students got is the ability to practice an unscripted verbal negotiation. And the best part is the GPT also gives them immediate feedback when they finish their exercise so they get immediate personalized feedback on what they've done. This is, um, obviously a very specific application in patent law, but you could use this in any number of interviewing or counseling scenarios, uh, for all kinds of classes. Any situation where a student has to think on their feet and interact with another party can be very realistically simulated with a custom GPT now. So let me show you behind the scenes how I did this and then I'm gonna give you a demo so you can see what it was like from the student's perspective to experience this. So first, from um, in terms of how I created it, I started by creating a detailed examiner persona. The examiner in my scenario is Carrie McNally. I created a persona and I used chat GPT to help me create this persona. I created a persona that defined her working style, her role, her level of authority at the US Patent Office, her expertise, put all of that into a persona for her. And then I created a custom GPT. So let me show you my GPT. Here's the configuration screen for my GPT. Can see down here in the documents in the knowledge base, I have the persona for Carrie McNally that I created, and then all the other documents that are part of the case file that the students have for this exercise. And then here's my instructions. They start off with, um, some basics just to make sure the students know how to activate voice mode 'cause that's critical here. And then there's two parts, the instructions for conducting the interview. And then the second part are the instructions for, um, the feedback portion of the exercise. So the instructions for conducting the interview are very general. I did not create a rigid script or a rigid list of questions. Instead, I generally told it how to behave so that it reacts and responds to what the student's saying and makes this a very dynamic experience. With respect to the feedback portion, I created a rubric and I gave the GPT the categories of my rubric so that it would be giving the students feedback on the things that I would give them feedback on if I was evaluating this exercise. Now. Now this didn't take me long to create this exercise, but my students really got a significantly improved experience from it. They got to, all of them, got to play the role of the patent attorney. They got to have a very realistic experience as to what an examiner interview is like. They got personalized and immediate feedback on how they performed. And I got visibility into each one of their negotiations because they can send me a link to their conversation in chat GPT. Even though this is being done with voice mode behind the scenes, it's still a conversation or a chat in chat GPT and there's a transcript of it and you, there's a, there's a link. They can send me a link and share that link so that I can see what they did. Now let me show you a couple of demos so you can see what it was like from the student's perspective. The first demo is from the interview portion of the exercise. Here we go. Hi, I'm primary examiner Carrie McNally. I understand that you wanna discuss application 17 6 78 8 67. Which specific claims and references would you like to focus on today? I'd like to discuss claim one and the Martin reference. Alright, I'd like to dive into Claim one and the Martin reference. I'll give you some constructive feedback on how you did based on a few categories. First off, in terms of clarity of your invention explanation, you did a nice job laying out the key distinction, especially around the circuit board element. You kept it pretty clear and not overly technical, which is great. On mastery of claim language, you did refer back to the exact wording of claim one to support your argument, and that's always a strong move. It showed you knew the claim language well. As for distinguishing over Martin, you did a good job focusing on a specific technical difference. You might wanna add a bit way detail about why a person of ordinary skill wouldn't just add that circuit boy to Martin, but you're on the right track on the dependent claim fallback strategy. Since you didn't propose amendments or discuss dependent claims, that's something to consider next time using them can sometimes strengthen your position in terms of responsiveness and adaptability. You adapted well to my questions and followed the flow of the conversation smoothly and your professional demeanor was on point, polite, confident, and respectful, which is exactly what you want in an examiner interview. Overall, you did a solid job. My main suggestion would be to think about bringing up dependent claims or possible amendments a bit more proactively next time.
